Forgent’s IPO Plan

FPS intends to sell shares of Class A common stock in an IPO likely to be substantially higher than its placeholder $100 million figure.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, at least as of the first IPO prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders will also have one vote per share, but will not be entitled to any distributions or dividends.

The Continuing Equity Owners (from the various underlying LLC operating companies and controlled by Neos) will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to Forgent’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds we receive from this offering to indirectly purchase [an as yet undisclosed amount of] Opco LLC Interests ...or Opco LLC Interests if the underwriters exercise in full their option to purchase additional shares of Class A common stock) from Opco at a price per unit equal to the initial public offering price per share of Class A common stock in this offering less the underwriting discounts and commissions.

Opco intends to use the approximately $ [undisclosed amount] in net proceeds it receives from the sale of Opco LLC Interests to us to redeem Opco LLC Interests from the Existing Opco LLC Owners at a price per unit equal to the initial public offering price per share of Class A common stock in this offering less the underwriting discounts and commissions and any withholding taxes.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said there are currently no legal claims that would have a material adverse effect on its business or financial condition.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Goldman Sachs, Jefferies, Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan, BofA Securities, Barclays, TD Cowen, MUFG, Wolfe | Nomura Alliance, KeyBanc Capital Markets and Oppenheimer & Co.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of September 30, 2025, Forgent had $81.4 million in cash and equivalents and $993.4 million in total liabilities, of which $501 million was debt.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, of negative ($72 million) on $106 million of capital expenditures.

FPS said it is coming to the conclusion of a multi-year $205 million initiative to grow its manufacturing capacity to be able to support up to $5 billion in total revenues.

So, with a lot of moving parts, from its significant M&A activity to its subsequent capital expenditure-intensive period, determining shareholder friendliness is difficult.

The firm’s net debt to EBITDA multiple is 2.9x, which is higher than public peers, but once the heavy CapEx is over, the free cash flow should return to positive and make this less of a worry.

So, the company is essentially still in "build" mode, so capital allocation looks less than ideal, although that should change significantly starting with 2026’s financial results.

Forgent Is Growing through M&A and Capacity Expansion

Fogent is seeking to go public in another private equity firm IPO early in 2026.

FPS’s financials have generated top line revenue growth from M&A activity, increasing gross profit but decreasing gross margin, higher operating profit and operating margin and fluctuating cash flow from operations.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenue has grown, and its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple fell to 2.4x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no cash dividends to holders of Class A shares.

The company’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as part of its plan to substantially expand its manufacturing capacity by the end of calendar 2026.

As to unit economics, Forgent’s primary focus has been on selling custom solutions, which generally produce higher gross margins than standardized products.

However, the firm’s growth strategy includes continuing to expand its manufacturing capacity to capture market share from capacity-limited competitors, expanding its prefab solutions, and introducing new products focused on the higher power density/data center markets to take advantage of the AI infrastructure buildout.

So, this growth strategy may result in lower overall gross margins going forward, but higher market share and gross profit.

Backlog looks quite strong, at just over $1 billion as of September 30, 2025, up 44% YoY and a backlog-to-revenue coverage of 1.36x.

The market opportunity for providing electrical distribution equipment in North America is large and is expected to grow significantly in the coming years, so the firm enjoys positive growth dynamics in its primary market.

The company has manufacturing concentration risks in North America as well as customer concentration risks in one region.

It also has supplier concentration risks for certain specialized components and materials.

It is designated as an ‘emerging growth company’, which enables management to produce less information for shareholders.

With a current revenue run rate of above $1 billion and increasing capacity along with plans to expand further into prefabricated systems, Forgent looks well-positioned to take advantage of the strong growth outlook for North American data center and grid modernization growth in the coming years.

There will likely be significant institutional investor demand for the IPO, assuming a reasonable valuation.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more about the IPO from management.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Steady 7% Yield Amid A Volatile Energy Backdrop
data.description:
It has been a tough last 12 months for owners of high-yield energy midstream partnerships. Key ETFs tracking the space have produced mid-single-digit total returns since mid-January 2025, all while global equities have enjoyed a torrid rally since April. The S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has returned 21% YoY, while ex-U.S. shares are up almost 40%, dividends included.

Today, I’m revisiting Enterprise Products Partners L.P. Common Units (EPD). I was bullish on the units back in October 2024. Since then, the return has been impressive—up more than 20%, actually outperforming SPY by a modest margin. Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. I’ll offer a refreshed valuation and an updated look at the technicals.

MLPs Lagging the Global Equity Market YoY, Weak Energy Sector In General

Back in October, EPD reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.61 missed the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.68, while revenue of $12.0 billion, down almost 13% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $220 million beat. Its adjusted cash flow from operations (CFFO) summed to $2.1 billion over the July through September stretch, which was about unchanged from 2024.

Units actually traded modestly higher after the mixed report, rising 1.5%--the best reaction to earnings going back at least three years, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services. Looking ahead to the Feb. 3 Q4 report, the options market prices in a low 2.7% share price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Units of the now $69 billion energy sector company feature a low 15% implied volatility, while short interest on EPD is modest at 1.34%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the $2.4 billion of adjusted EBITDA was a bit shy of expectations, and the management team was less upbeat compared to after the Q2 update. What’s more, executives were somewhat cautious on the future growth outlook, perhaps due to delays in commissioning projects like the Bahia pipeline. The good news was that the partnership increased its buyback program to $5 billion, a sort of bullish body language move that was perhaps sparked by the post-earnings rally.

On the call, EPD expressed optimism that it would return to target return levels by the end of this year, helped by new project EBITDA contribution, while a steady capex forecast should help ensure strong free cash flow generation. Still, volatility in the oil and gas market (as we have seen in the past few months) was cited as a possible headwind.

For unitholders, the partnership continued its disciplined capital return strategy in Q3 2025, distributing $0.545 per unit (up 3.8% YoY) and repurchasing $80 million of common units. The company also maintained its strong financial health with a 3.3x leverage ratio (targeting about 3.0x), $3.6 billion in liquidity, and an adjusted CFFO payout ratio of 58%. EPD’s total gross operating margin was $2.39 billion in Q3 2025, down modestly from $2.45 billion in Q3 2024 and $2.48 billion sequentially.

EPD hiked its dividend on Thursday, Jan. 9, 2026, while before that, the firm announced a partnership with Exxon Mobil (XOM), forming a joint venture to expand EPD’s Bahia pipeline.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to verify negative for CY 2025, but then inflect positive for 2026 and through the out year. Revenue growth could also turn positive, but weakness in the oil and gas market could result in further earnings downgrades.

Indeed, there have been a high 10 bottom-line negative revisions in the past 90 days compared to just a lone upgrade. The good news is that EPD is highly profitable, now with $1.43 of free cash flow per share over the past 12 months.

EPD: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, Mixed EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my Q4 2024 analysis. If we assume normalized per-unit earnings of $2.85 (slightly below the current consensus, given recent softer trends in EPD’s EBITDA revisions) and apply a 12x forward multiple, then units should trade near $34.

That multiple is appropriate if long-term earnings growth steadies in the mid-high single digits. Bear in mind that unitholders are paid to wait, now that the yield is 6.9%.

EPD: Near the Long-Term Average P/E, High Yield

Key risks for EPD include rising competition across domestic basins, namely the Permian, while regional operational risks were particularly called out by analysts on the Q3 earnings call.

And while the leverage situation looks fine to me, the street has grown mildly worried about the capex allocation strategy regarding free cash flow for buybacks versus debt repayment. Of course, lower oil and gas prices (and domestic demand) could weigh on the top line in the quarters ahead.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, Feb. 3, BMO. Before that, units pay a $0.55 distribution on Friday, Jan. 30.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

While units remain a solid value in my estimate, EPD’s technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that it has been a sideways and choppy trade for the better part of the last year. Resistance is apparent at the $33 mark, while support is close to $30. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a neutral zone from 30 to 70.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s now falling, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. The upside there is that a bullish golden cross pattern played out just recently, when the 50dma climbed above the 200dma. Units are also above both moving averages—a good sign. Long-term resistance is near $34.50, while an uptrend support line comes into the scene at $29.

EPD: Neutral Trading Range, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on EPD. I see units as undervalued enough to remain bullish on the risk/reward profile, while the near-$7 yield is quite attractive as Treasury yields potentially venture lower as the year progresses.

data.title: Flutter Entertainment: Prediction Market Is Lucrative, But Competition Is Fierce
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, Flutter Entertainment plc (FLUT) said its share price went back to the $200s, so I thought I’d go through its performance over the last while and give some comments on what the best opportunity for it is going forward. Prediction markets look promising, but the management’s plan of rolling it out slowly may not be proactive enough to beat out the competition.

Briefly On Performance

Starting from the top, we can see that the company’s revenues over the last two years have been steadily growing. On average, the company saw its revenue increase by around 13% a quarter, which is rather respectable. The most recent quarter, Q3 ’25, showed growth accelerating to 17% y/y, which slightly beat estimates by around $13m. The international revenue segment saw fantastic growth of around 21%, while the U.S. showed around 9%. iGaming revenue in the U.S., however, grew a whopping 44% y/y. Casino operations are doing phenomenally well.

Over the same timeframe, I am a little disappointed to see that the company’s margins across the board saw very little change. It seems that the company is betting heavily on the U.S. markets and is investing a lot of money in growth, like FanDuel Predicts and other M&As. I hope that it will work out in the end.

It also seems that on a GAAP basis, the company is still not profitable. This past quarter, the company was particularly deep in the red according to the GAAP reporting, as its GAAP EPS came in at a loss of $3.91 a share due to a massive goodwill impairment charge related to the ban of online real money gaming in India. The company wrote off all of the operations there.

It’s not a massive hit to the company, as Junglee was expected to bring around $200m full-year 2025 revenue, or around 1% of total revenue.

Additionally, it seems like the management isn’t doing a particularly good job of utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital. Since the company’s net income (GAAP) is negative, so are ROA and ROE. The company’s ROTC, which I like to look at to see if the company has any pricing power and a moat, is very low, too. Well below 10%, I like to see at a minimum. What’s worse is that all these metrics began to decline at the beginning of 2025.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. As of Q3 ’25, the company had around $1.7B in cash and equivalents, against $12B in long-term debt. The company secured decent covenants on the massive debt pile, needing to have a net debt to EBITDA ratio of less than 5.2x, and right now, it’s at around 4x, with S&P Global (SPGI) rating its debt as low risk at Bbb3. Nothing matures until 2028, so there is also a lot of time to come up with the capital necessary. Operating cash flow for the nine months ended September 30th was down to $756m from $950m, but that is still enough to cover any interest expenses it may have, as well as its decent cash pile. I don’t see any liquidity or solvency risks.

Overall, I don’t see anything exciting regarding the company’s performance over the last couple of years. It has been struggling to turn a GAAP profit as its operating expenses continue to outweigh the revenue growth. However, I hope that is going to change in the next while, otherwise, the company will remain unattractive to me.

Predictions Markets Boom

Prediction markets essentially came out of nowhere, and apparently, it is not called betting. If it were betting, many states, including the biggest ones in the U.S. will not allow it to be a thing. So, what’s the difference? Predictions markets seem to be very similar to a derivatives contract you may find with your favorite company. Let’s take an example of an options contract.

Let’s look at a call example. If you think a company’s share is going to be over $50 in a month, then you purchase a call; you say, “Yes, I believe that to be true.” Another person might not be this optimistic and can sell you this contract, saying, “No, I don’t think so.” That is essentially what prediction markets are. There are scenarios based on whatever is available in the world, and people can make predictions on whether that event will happen or not. The structure differs in a way that in gambling, there are fixed-odds against the house, or bookies, whereas prediction markets are peer-to-peer and are regulated by the CME (CME), which acts as the Designated Contract Market and a clearing house for FanDuel Predicts, which is Flutter’s new revenue segment that seems to be very promising.

Polymarket and Kalshi are currently dominating this space, but many players are beginning to enter the space, like Robinhood (HOOD), DraftKings (DKNG), and Flutter. These companies already have a massive customer base, so it is not going to be as hard to get into the market, especially since prediction markets are not seen as gambling, which means this can be made available nationally in all 50 states. Flutter is taking it slow, by first offering it in 5 states so far. The company is testing the waters in Alabama, Alaska, North Carolina, North Dakota, and South Dakota.

These states, as far as I know, do not allow online sports betting, so it seems that the company is taking a very cautious step forward. It is a lot slower than what its rival, DKNG, has done recently. It went ahead and offered prediction markets to 38 states at the end of December, which are a mix of non-betting states (17 states) and betting states. The non-betting states include Texas and California, which are massive.

This seems to be the only exciting opportunity for the company right now. With the Nevada Gaming Control Board enforcing bans on unlicensed sports predictions in 2025, companies like FLUT and DKNG withdrew sportsbook apps to launch Predictions instead. So, we can see where these companies see the future heading. I think they are going about it a bit too slowly. DKNG is taking aggressive steps to cement itself as one of the dominant players in the space, so it will be interesting to see how this plan is going to turn out.

Right now, all the focus of predictions market is on the U.S. region, but I believe having a presence in the UK and Ireland through subsidiaries like Sky Bet, Paddy Power, and Betfair could potentially unlock a much higher TAM opportunity. No plans as of yet, of course, but it’s something worth considering, especially if the full launch goes well in the U.S.

Closing Comments

Right now, I’m not itching to spend any money on Flutter Entertainment’s hope of successfully launching the prediction market app. The big players like Polymarket and Kalshi have a massive lead here, and the websites offer so many more opportunities compared to what DraftKings or FanDuel offers right now that it is hard to recommend buying any of these two right now, just on the hopes that there is enough room for all to exist.

DraftKings is being a bit more proactive when it comes to this opportunity. Both of these companies just launched the product, so I will be interested to see what kind of numbers they will do in the next quarter’s report. Is it the company’s ticket to consistent profitability in the future? We’ll have to wait and see. FLUT’s shares are a lot more attractive after such a massive dip recently, but I need to see the potential of prediction markets before I commit.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ZIM Integrated: Takeover Uncertainties Met With Muted Volatility - Buy Call Options
data.description:
ZIM stock: takeover developments

My last analysis on ZIM Integrated Shipping Services Ltd. (NYSE:ZIM) was published on Nov 25 under a title of “ZIM Integrated Q3: Let Your Profit Run.” The article rated the stock as a Buy and other basic information of the article was shown in the screenshot below. Since then, there have been a few material developments surrounding this Israeli shipping company. In the rest of this follow-up article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: the ongoing takeover discussions and the change of its IV (implied volatility) in the option market. My thesis is that the moderated IV underestimates the true uncertainties, thus providing a favorable setup for a long call option strategy.

Shortly since my last writing, ZIM announced that it received a take private offer from company CEO and President Eli Glickman and Rami Unger. Shortly after that, ZIM board also announced the receival of other takeover offers/interests (see more details of these developments quoted below). To further compound the uncertainty, the Israeli Companies Authority also entered the picture as "the state has the right to oppose" a potential sale of more than 24% of ZIM's shares.

ZIM press release (Dec 22, 2025): As part of the review of strategic alternatives that has been on-going during the past several months, the ZIM Board of Directors received competitive proposals from multiple strategic parties to acquire all the outstanding ordinary shares of the Company, and it is currently evaluating such proposals with a focus on delivering significant value to all the Company's shareholders. The ZIM Board of Directors has also received a revised proposal from an entity owned by Eli Glickman, the Company's Chief Executive Officer and President, and Rami Ungar. After careful consideration, the ZIM Board of Directors concluded that this proposal significantly undervalued the Company and informed the management-led entity that its revised proposal was declined.

ZIM stock: implied volatility in the option market

I expect these above uncertainties to heighten ZIM’s share price fluctuations in the near future, which have already been quite wild historically due to the highly cyclical nature of its earnings to begin with (more on this in a minute). Yet, the stock’s IV is quite muted in the option market.

To wit, the following data were taken from MarketChameleon and present the current IV of ZIM together with some historical context. As seen, the current IV is only 51.5, lower than the 52-week average of 56.9 by about 10%. It is also about 12% below its 20-day moving average of 58.4 and about 10% below its 20-day HV (historical volatility of 56.9). Expanding the horizon wider, the current IV is also about 12% below the 252-day HV of 58.4).

Next, I will explain why the combination of muted IV and the rue underlying uncertainties provides a favorable setup to go long on its call options.

ZIM stock: options are attractively priced

To better contextualize the details of the call options, let’s take quick digression and look at ZIM’s recent and income earnings. As seen in the snapshot below, ZIM released its last earnings report (ER) on Nov 20, 2025, and the upcoming ER is scheduled for Mar 12, 2026. In the last quarter, ZIM earned an EPS Of $1.03 on non-GAAP basis. In contrast, analysts’ latest EPS revisions (with 2 downward revisions and no upward revisions) point to a loss of $0.99 per share. Such extreme earnings swing is not only limited to the next quarter, but is observed quite often as seen in the next chart. As seen, in the past 5 quarters, the company’s EPS varied from a peak of $9.34 to $0.19, and its sales exhibited YOY changes from +117% to -35%.

Such large volatility in its financials have caused large price volatilities in the past, and I expect high – of further heightened – volatility in the near future with the ongoing takeover discussions. As such, I believe the current IV underestimate the true share price uncertainties and provides a favorable option setup for buyers. As a specific example, let me draw your attention to a call option with an expiration date of April 17, 2026, and a strike of $23. This option has an expiry after ZIM’s next ER scheduled on March 12, and thus can help accommodate surprises in the ER (due to earnings, takeover, or other geopolitical risks).

As seen, the option last traded with a premium of $1.65 per share only. The premium is about 7% of ZIM’s current share price of $22.4, allowing potential investors to express their bullish view with a relatively small capital layout compared to direct share ownership.

Also note that these NTM (near-the-money) options have attracted a sizable investors to help with liquidity. The option with a $23 strike has an open interest of more than 5,000 and a volume of 88 in the last trading session.

Other risks and final thoughts

To echo a point mentioned earlier, ZIM’s wild earnings swings is due to the fundamental of its business and is a structural risk that investors – either option traders or share owners – need to stomach. Looking past the quarterly fluctuations as shown above, the next chart shows its EPS estimates in the next 3 years. You see the same pattern here with extremely volatile year-over-year EPS changes. ZIM’s extreme EPS volatility is primarily a results of the fundamentals of the shipping sector, the sector’s sensitivity to geological events, and also the uniqueness of ZIM’s own business model. For example, ZIM’s earnings in recent quarters have been benefiting from the geopolitical disruptions – substantially in my view – in the Red Sea area. The disruptions have artificially constrained capacity and pushed freight rates higher, and even degree, outcome, and duration of the disruptions are ultimately unpredictable.

To compensate for the business uncertainties, ZIM is currently trading at exceptionally low valuation multiples compared to historical norms and industry peers. Specifically, the stock carries a TTM P/E ratio of only 2.7 and FWD ratio is only 8.26 as seen in the chart below. This represents a significant discount from the shipping industry average. However, due to the extreme volatility of its earnings, investors need to bear in mind the limited leading signal that its headline P/E ratios offer. But even in terms of price-to-book ratio, which is less affected by cyclical forces and/or geological risks, the stock is also attractively valued. As seen, its current P/B ratios sit at approximately 0.67 as of TTM and at 0.74 on an FWD basis, suggesting a sizable discount of its book value. Historically, the stock traded at an average P/B ratio of 1.46 in the past five years.

To wrap up, this ZIM follow-up is promoted mainly by two changes since my last writing: the takeover discussion and the changes of its IV In the option market. My assessment is that the current IV underestimates the true uncertainties underlying its business fundamentals and share prices. This discrepancy, when combined with the valuation multiples (especially in terms of book value discount), provides a favorable setup to go long on its call options.

data.title: Mondelez: Sweet Bargain For Dividend Investors
data.description:
It pays to be patient, especially when it comes to consumer staples stocks with durable brands. While near-term headwinds can cause price swings, patient investors can use market fear to their advantage by taking a long-term view. As Warren Buffett once aptly put it: “Successful investing takes time, discipline, and patience. No matter how great the talent or effort, some things just take time.”

This brings me to Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ), which I last covered in October, highlighting its robust top-line growth and diversified global portfolio of brands. MDLZ continues to trade in value territory at the current price of $55.09, which is close to its 52-week low of $51. It also carries a 3.6% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit MDLZ, including recent business results, and discuss why negative market sentiment on this stock has created a great buying opportunity for long-term value and income investors, so let’s get started!

Why MDLZ?

Mondelez is a global snacking giant with a presence in over 150 countries. Its portfolio of iconic brands includes Oreo, Ritz, Clif Bar, Cadbury Dairy Milk, and Toblerone chocolate. Over the trailing 12 months, MDLZ generated $37.6 billion in total revenue.

Those familiar with MDLZ may know that its results over the past few quarters have been affected by higher cocoa prices. This has led to margin and EPS pressure on the company. However, it's worth noting that the top line has continued to steadily grow since 2024 after a significant ramp-up in the years before that, as shown below.

MDLZ continued its recent track record of top-line growth during Q3 2025, with organic revenue rising by 3.4% YoY. This was driven by price increases, which more than offset a volume decline of 4.6% from the prior year period. This means that consumers were able to absorb at least some of the cost from higher cocoa prices. Emerging markets drove most of the growth, with organic revenue rising by 7.1% YoY, while developed markets organic revenue rose by just 1.2% YoY, as shown below.

MDLZ’s chocolate prices have risen by 30% over the past 12 reported months, and while demand has been relatively steady, management is seeing pockets of pressure. On the other hand, biscuit, cakes, and pastries demand has remained stable. This was particularly the case in Europe, where these products have all gained market share and volume growth.

Notably, price elasticity for MDLZ’s products in Europe was around 0.75, which is higher than management expectations of 0.4 to 0.5. For reference, a price elasticity of below 1 means that consumers are not as price sensitive, so the lower the score, the better. As such, MDLZ is working to address this issue by innovating into new flavors and formats while hitting on the right price points.

Looking ahead to Q4 results and beyond, management is guiding for full-year 2025 organic revenue growth of 4% and adjusted EPS decline of 15% from the prior year, due primarily to higher cocoa prices. Nonetheless, MDLZ still expects to maintain $3 billion in free cash flow for the full year, due in part to disciplined capital expense management. This would be on par with $3.055 billion in free cash flow generated in the prior year.

Moreover, MDLZ could see success in future zero-sugar offerings like what consumer staples giant Coca-Cola (KO) has seen with Coke Zero. This is reflected by MDLZ’s work in expanding the zero Oreo range as well as the gluten-free Oreo. Plus, protein continues to have its moment with consumers, and MDLZ is well-positioned with the Perfect Bar and BUILDERS, both of which have seen 20%+ growth.

Importantly, management expects to have passed the peak in cocoa prices, which means that it’s on track for better earnings growth in 2026. As shown below, cocoa futures have declined significantly and have dipped to $5,312 to start off the new year. While it still has a ways to go to reach the $2,500 level, the recent price actions are a move in the right direction.

MDLZ maintains a solid BBB credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $1.37 billion in cash on the balance sheet. While the net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio is moderately elevated at 3.66x, this has more to do with margin pressures from higher cocoa prices. With lower prices on the horizon, I would expect the leverage ratio to trend down this year closer to 3x.

Importantly for income investors, MDLZ currently yields 3.6%, and the dividend is well covered by a 67% payout ratio. The dividend also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 10.1% and 12 consecutive years of growth. As shown below, MDLZ’s TTM yield is at the highest point in at least 10 years.

Lastly, I find value in MDLZ at the current price of $55.09 with a forward PE of 19.0. While this valuation doesn’t scream bargain, it’s worth noting that this is because of the aforementioned expected decline in earnings last year. Analysts who follow the company expect a rebound in EPS this year and estimate 8% to 10% EPS growth annually over the next 4 years. As shown below, this could push the P/E ratio below 15 by 2028.

Plus, even as MDLZ’s forward P/E is elevated due to lower earnings in 2025, it still sits slightly below its historical P/E of 19.4, as shown below. As such, it appears the market is not baking in expectations for a turnaround in earnings this year.

Risks to MDLZ include its exposure to cocoa prices. Unforeseen structural cocoa shortages due to weather and crop disease in West Africa could have an outsized impact on the earnings outlook. Continued price hikes could result in more price elasticity risk as consumers trade down into other confectionery categories. Shifting consumer tastes away from packaged and sugary goods could pressure volume growth. Plus, a stronger U.S. dollar would translate to currency risk from MDLZ’s international revenues.

Investor Takeaway

Mondelez remains a high-quality consumer staples name with durable brands, global scale, and dependable cash flow, despite near-term earnings pressure from elevated cocoa costs. The market’s current pessimism has pushed the stock near multi-year lows and lifted the dividend yield to an attractive level. This is even as management maintains strong free cash flow and a solid balance sheet.

MDLZ’s growth is driven by emerging markets and innovation, and cocoa prices are showing signs of easing. As such, it appears positioned for an earnings rebound in 2026. For long-term value and income investors willing to look past short-term volatility, the current valuation offers a compelling entry point into a proven wealth compounder. For these reasons, I maintain a "Buy" rating on the stock.

data.title: MoneyShow's Best Investment Ideas For 2026: Part 3
data.description:
MoneyShow’s top contributing analysts, strategists, and newsletter editors share their investment recommendations for the year ahead. See Part 2 here.

Celldex Therapeutics Inc.

By John McCamant, Editor of Medical Technology Stock Letter

We are recommending Celldex Therapeutics Inc. (CLDX) as a more conservative biotech Top Pick for 2026. CLDX has just initiated the second Phase III/registration study with its lead compound barzolvolimab (“barzo”) in chronic inducible urticaria (CIndU).

This study will be identical to the Phase III CSU design, including a booster initial dose that will most likely improve efficacy during the start-up phase of the dosing, a well-known practice often performed with injectable monoclonal antibodies (MAbs). In our view, barzo is positioned to treat multiple mast cell diseases, making it a pipeline in a drug - and a drug that will also command premium pricing.

Consensus by Wall Street models is using roughly $40,000 annual pricing for barzo. CLDX management has stated that because barzo efficacy lasts well more after stopping a year of dosing (76 weeks and counting), pricing and reimbursement is looking to be much higher than the consensus believes. For example, Dupixent is over $60,000 per year. Hence, we think the Street’s CLDX models and forecasts will move higher over time... and so should CLDX stock target prices.

With two Phase III trials for CIndU and CSU, barzo is already a pipeline in a drug. In our view, the next blockbuster indication for barzo will be food allergies. The door is wide open now with Novartis' (NVS) Xolair gaining real traction on its recent food allergies FDA approvals/label expansion. CLDX will likely start a food allergy trial with barzo in 2026.

As a reminder, barzo in CSU works where Xolair does and does not and, most importantly, in Xolair refractory patients, too. Food allergies make perfect sense for the next barzo mega indication. In addition to CSU, CIndU, PN (prurigo nodularis), AD (atopic dermatitis), and then food allergies will help barzo become a biotech blockbuster.

The differences between barzo and the company’s next mast cell MAb ‘622 is the binding and inhibition of stem cell factor (SCF - membrane expressed vs. soluble ‘248 which is blocked by CDX-622). Mast cells inhibited by ‘248 are responsible for inflammation/allergy conditions (vs. membrane, which is hematopoietic/cancer-related conditions and may have more A/Es).

The combination of SCF/TSLP (the other specific target in this bispecific MAb) is non-redundant and will hit different mast cell populations. In the healthy volunteer trials released in late 2025, tryptase was blocked 50% at the highest doses tested. It’s still early, but there is excitement as the company expects to start trials in asthma soon and then other indications.

Recommended Action: Buy CLDX.

Ceragon Networks

By Philip MacKeller, Editor of Contra the Heard

Ceragon Networks Ltd. (CRNT) had a rough 2025, tumbling by more than half. But the stock also had an incredible 2024, rising over 116%. All in all, Ceragon looks primed for long-term growth... and is supported by strong macro drivers and an attractive valuation. That makes it my top growth pick for 2026.

Ceragon is a small-cap play on 5G and other communication technologies, providing wireless products and services that support wireless and cellular service providers. Headquartered in Israel and listed in the US, the company does business globally. Last quarter, India accounted for 29% of sales, while North America made up 31% of revenue. The remainder was split between Latin America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, and Africa.

The enterprise is well-positioned to benefit from strong underlying trends. According to Ericsson’s latest Mobility Report, India’s 5G adoption rate is currently around 32% but should increase to 79% by 2031. North America, already a global leader, should see an expansion from 79% to 92%.

Not only is 5G usage growing, but companies, governments, and large users are building out their own private networks, adding to demand for Ceragon’s products and services. CRNT stands to benefit from geopolitical and trade tensions with Huawei and China as well.

In addition to these macro drivers, the corporation is healthy. The balance sheet is clean, customer concentrations are low, and insiders own around 10% of shares, aligning them with the interests of other owners. Best of all, the company is cheap. Many valuation measures are well off their five-year averages, and on certain metrics, CRNT is trading at cycle lows.

Ceragon’s weak share price performance comes down to India, where many projects have been delayed. A large customer has also faced financial difficulty. Though purchasing activity among Indian carriers improved in the latest quarter, this remains a risk heading into 2026.

Other concerns include shareholder dilution, stiff competition, and a patchy earnings history. M&A integration or overpaying are risks as well. The recent purchases of Siklu and E2E Technologies have gone smoothly, but Ceragon could bite off more than they can chew in the future. It is also a small-cap stock based in Israel.

That said, I still like the name for 2026.

Recommended Action: Buy CRNT.

Constellation Energy Corp.

By Carl Delfeld, Editor of Cabot Explorer

Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG) is an Artificial Intelligence (AI) infrastructure power play and the largest owner of nuclear power plants in the US. Based on forward earnings expectations, the stock is priced well below high-profile AI stocks, providing an attractive risk/reward profile and thus a more conservative play on AI trends.

CEG is both a regulated utility and one that sells power on the wholesale market. Importantly, this means Constellation operates in the unregulated price utility market, so it can charge any rate the market can bear. While data and semiconductor chips are often thought of as the most important AI fuel, the tech industry has a nearly infinite demand for 24-hour-a-day power for AI data centers.

The US has added only three new, big nuclear reactors since the 1990s. But the tide has turned as demand for more output and lower prices at home has soared. In addition, China has become a leader in nuclear technology, with about 100 nuclear power plants in their pipeline.

Constellation currently owns and operates 21 nuclear reactors as well as hydro and wind power facilities. Furthermore, an impending all-stock acquisition deal with Calpine will add geothermal and natural gas to the menu. When this deal is completed, Constellation will have the capacity to provide electricity to about 40 million homes.

Highlighting bipartisan support for more safe nuclear power, the Department of Energy is approving a $1 billion federal loan to restart the Three Mile Island nuclear power plant in Pennsylvania that suffered a partial core meltdown in 1979.

Constellation’s latest quarterly earnings demonstrated fundamental momentum, with adjusted operating earnings hitting $1.91 per share, up 20%.

Recommended Action: Buy CEG.

Coppernico Metals Inc.

By Omar Ayales, Editor of Gold Charts R Us

As gold and precious metals enjoy a strong cycle, history shows leadership often shifts toward industrial metals - especially copper. As this rotation unfolds, junior explorers positioned in prospective districts could see powerful upside. Coppernico Metals Inc. (CPPMF) is one to keep on the radar.

Coppernico Metals is a mineral exploration company focused on discovering large copper-gold systems in South America. Its flagship Sombrero Project in southern Peru hosts extensive skarn and porphyry potential.

The company has been active: expanding concessions, securing drill permits, and advancing systematic exploration across multiple targets. A 2024 financing - with participation from a strategic investor - adds credibility to its long-term plans.

Coppernico is a junior explorer. That means no production, no operating cash flow, and a reliance on continued financing and exploration success. Dilution risk is real, and many juniors never reach production.

But that’s also where the highest risk/highest reward opportunities tend to emerge. If capital rotates from precious to industrial metals, copper juniors could move sharply.

Why 2026 Could Be a Good Year

Industrial Metals Momentum: Copper demand continues to strengthen. Underinvestment, tightening supply, electrification, grid upgrades, renewable infrastructure, and EV growth all point to a firm long-term market.

Rotation Setup: Copper remains extremely undervalued relative to gold - levels that historically have preceded multi-year reversals.

Exploration Catalysts: With active permits, expanded targets, and fresh financing, Coppernico appears primed for drilling campaigns that could generate news flow, results, and possibly partnership activity.

Coppernico should be viewed as risk capital. Volatility is high, and outcomes hinge on drilling success. But the asymmetry is attractive: limited downside outside of dilution and failed results, with significant upside if discoveries materialize or the industrial metals cycle accelerates.

Bottom line: Coppernico Metals fits the profile of a high-risk/high-reward speculative play for 2026 - a leveraged bet on rising copper demand, a potential sector rotation, and exploration breakthroughs. If industrial metals lead the next commodity wave, this stock could deliver outsized gains. Just be prepared for volatility and the all-or-nothing nature of junior mining.

Recommended Action: Buy CPPMF.

Costco Wholesale Corp.

By Joe Markman, Editor at Digital Creators & Consumers

Costco Wholesale Corp. (COST) is a stock that features a powerful narrative: its substantial, long-term structural advantages are being obscured by investor concerns over immediate sales growth. It offers compelling value because the market is failing to appreciate the true scale of its opportunity.

Costco is sitting on a massive, multi-year physical footprint opportunity. Accelerating demographic tailwinds should define the company’s next decade of growth.

We view the company as a premium subscription service that generates revenue from the sale of bulk goods. In fiscal year 2025, the company delivered strong results with $269.9 billion in net sales, up 8.1% year-over-year. This financial strength is underpinned by an exceptional 91% membership renewal rate. Subscriptions are a stable, high-margin, recurring revenue stream.

However, the critical opportunity being missed by investors is the scope of Costco’s underleveraged footprint. The company operates only 914 warehouses globally. This pales in comparison to competitors. For instance, Walmart Inc. (WMT) has over 10,750 locations.

This vast disparity highlights the significant white space available for expansion, particularly as the company targets affluent, suburban shoppers. Management plans 35 new warehouses in fiscal 2026. This acceleration in store openings is perfectly aligned with the swelling of affluent suburbs globally.

Digital growth further compounds this advantage. E-commerce sales surged by 27% in 2024, driven by big-ticket items fulfilled through Costco Logistics. Executives forecast returns on equity near 32%. The Costco story is about buying a high-quality subscription business with a massive, de-risked expansion runway at a standard retail valuation.

Recommended Action: Buy COST.

DigitalOcean Holdings Inc.

By Matthew Timpane, Editor at Schaeffer’s Investment Research

DigitalOcean Holdings Inc. (DOCN) enters 2026 with a powerful setup as AI shifts from experimentation to real deployment across startups and SMBs. Nvidia Corp.’s (NVDA) announcement that it will step away from operating its own cloud business is also an underappreciated catalyst.

Oracle Corp. (ORCL), Amazon Web Services ("AWS") (AMZN), and Azure (MSFT) will all benefit, but DigitalOcean is uniquely positioned as a developer-first alternative to hyperscalers. The company is now offering Nvidia H100 cloud GPUs and building an AI and LLM stack specifically designed for SMBs and AI-native teams.

If Nvidia begins directing customers toward partners instead of the DGX Cloud, DigitalOcean could experience a significant influx of new customers. The fundamental story also continues to improve. DigitalOcean is reaccelerating growth after a year of stabilization and product consolidation.

Higher-value Scalers+ customers now account for a growing share of revenue and have been expanding at a strong double-digit pace as the platform becomes more reliable, more capable, and better aligned with heavier AI workloads. AI services have grown triple digits for multiple quarters, supported by rising GPU capacity and expanding multi-year commitments from AI-native companies.

With better product velocity, improved balance sheet flexibility, and increasing visibility into customer expansion patterns, DigitalOcean is positioned to drive sustained mid-to-high teens growth as investors begin to re-rate the business.

From a technical perspective, the stock was recently sitting near the top of a stage 1 accumulation base, and a breakout could fuel a move of 50% or more in 2026. Price has reclaimed its Initial Public Offering (IPO) level, which now serves as support. The Fibonacci retracement levels from the 2021 highs to the 2023 lows create reasonable upside markers, with the 50% retracement at $76 and the 61.8% retracement at $89.50.

Options activity aligns with this view. We are seeing size buyers out in time at the October 60 strike, and based on the premium paid, they would be targeting a move of at least plus-50% into year-end. This is consistent with the developing technical structure and the improving fundamental backdrop.

Sentiment also sets up a favorable dynamic. Short interest is 14.5% of the stock’s available float and the highest it’s been since 2023, giving the stock potential for a short-covering rally as growth reacceleration takes hold. Options remain relatively inexpensive, with the equity’s Schaeffer’s Volatility Index (SVI) of 63% ranking in the 12th annual percentile. This signals subdued volatility expectations at a time when the company is entering a stronger phase of execution.

Recommended Action: Buy DOCN.

Dynex Capital Inc.

By Nancy Zambell, Editor-in-Chief of Cabot Money Club

Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) is a mortgage Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT), investing in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) in the US. The REIT’s portfolio consists of high-quality borrowers with only 2% of its MBS holdings yielding above 6%.

The real estate industry is seeing its fundamentals improving, including rising home inventory, stabilizing housing prices, and declining mortgage rates. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate was around 6.17% in late 2025, down almost a percentage point for the full year.

For its third quarter, Dynex saw significant earnings growth, up 120.9% over the past year, an impressive improvement from its five-year average decline of 26.8% per year. Additionally, the REIT’s net profit margin jumped to 76.1%.

The REIT is trading at a below-industry P/E, 11.5x, and its investors enjoy a very attractive dividend yield, recently 14.9%. That’s fantastic cash flow, in addition to potential share appreciation.

Originally posted on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Inhibrx: Ozekibart Beyond Chondrosarcoma, With Other Targeted Indications, Too
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Inhibrx Biosciences, Inc. (INBX) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Inhibrx: Positive Ozekibart Data For Bone Cancer Leads To Q2 2026 BLA Filing." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company had achieved positive results from its phase 2 registrational ChonDRAgon study, which evaluated the use of ozekibart [INBRX-109] in unresectable or metastatic conventional chondrosarcoma patients. Based on this, the company was planning to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] for ozekibart to treat these specific patients in Q2 of 2026.

Since my last article, the stock has traded higher by 26.37%. If such gains were made, then why do I believe that there is still shareholder value left for this company? Why would there be additional gains in the share price? The answer to both of these questions would be catalysts. One in particular is that the company has not yet filed its BLA of ozekibart for the treatment of these patients with unresectable or metastatic chondrosarcoma. As a matter of fact, it is still on track to file such a BLA in Q2 of 2026.

However, in this article I'm going to go over the second catalyst opportunity, which is a data readout. Inhibrx is in the process of evaluating the use of ozekibart in combination with FOLFIRI in heavily pretreated patients [who are 3rd-line/4th-line therapy] with advanced or metastatic, unresectable colorectal cancer in a phase 1/2 study. The importance of this program is that the company already released positive interim data in terms of response rate and durable responses observed.

Secondly, going back to the second catalyst I want to mention, it believes that it is on track to release progression-free survival [PFS] data in Q2 of 2026. There is a caveat here, because the company believes that data at that time will be mature enough to be in a position to release it. However, it is quite possible that it could slip further past this date to a later time. Regardless, the company should be able to release PFS data from this phase 1/2 study at some point during 2026.

Another solid program to mention briefly would be the development of ozekibart in combination with irinotecan and temozolomide [IRI/TMZ] for the treatment of patients with advanced/metastatic, unresectable Ewing Sarcoma [ES]. Enrollment completion for this program is expected in Q2 of 2026, and if the duration of response and response analysis trend well, then the company might be able to hold an FDA meeting in the 2nd half of 2026.

What would be the purpose of such a meeting? To discuss the potential of a possible Accelerated Pathway for FDA approval of ozekibart + IRI/TMZ for the treatment of these patients with unresectable, relapsed, or refractory ES. With the expected BLA filing of ozekibart for the treatment of patients with unresectable or metastatic conventional chondrosarcoma in Q2 of 2026, plus the PFS data readout from the phase 1/2 study using its drug ozekibart in combination with FOLFIRI for heavily pretreated 3rd-line/4th-line metastatic colorectal cancer [mCRC] patients during the same time period, I believe that I should maintain a Strong Buy rating on this stock.

PFS Data Is Another Catalyst Shoe To Drop That Could Potentially Boost Shareholder Value

As I noted above, Inhibrx Biosciences is in the process of gearing up to file its Biologics License Application [BLA] of ozekibart for the treatment of patients with unresectable or metastatic chondrosarcoma in Q2 of 2026. Another possible catalyst could happen at this time, as I will go over below. This particular program involves an ongoing phase 1/2 study using ozekibart [INBRX-109] as a tetravalent DR5 agonist in combination with FOLFIRI to treat 3rd-line/4th line advanced or metastatic, unresectable colorectal cancer [CRC]. Before going over this particular study, plus any catalysts relating to this program, I believe that it is important to go over what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Colorectal cancer [CRC] as the name suggests, is the growth of malignant cells in the colon or rectum of a patient. How does colon cancer actually come about? Well, it forms on the basis of abnormal and/or malignant cells growing uncontrollably in the patient's colon [large intestine]. However, in the case of this cancer, there might be a good way of preventing it. This would be to monitor for what are known as polyps. Such polyps form in a person's colon, and they are non-cancerous. On the flip side, some can end up becoming cancerous, and thus this brings forth CRC, or colon cancer. The age to screen for this type of cancer has changed from age "50" where it used to be, to age "45" now.

Okay, good, but what about the market opportunity here? There is a clear unmet medical need in targeting these metastatic patients. I call them "metastatic" CRC patients because they are heavily pretreated with many other prior therapies. First and foremost, consider that not many patients reach this stage to begin with, and there are not many effective treatment options for them. From what I could find, in real-world settings, CRC patients that reach each of these stages are as follows:

  • 25% to 30% of real-world 1st-line patients reach third-line therapy.
  • 10% to 15% obtain 4th-line therapy.

Current treatment options might be targeted therapies along with chemotherapy, but most notably these patients receive irinotecan or a combination of 5-fluorourarcil [5FU] + irinotecan. The global metastatic colorectal cancer market is expected to reach $19.12 billion by 2032. This is a good market opportunity for this company, and the hope is that it can do well in the unmet medical area of helping these heavily pretreated mCRC patients.

As I will note below, the company initiated an expansion cohort of 44 patients. The reason why I bring this up is because about 80% of them were treated with prior irinotecan therapy. These are not easy patients to treat at all, and the company still had some positive data to report. How does this drug work to help treat these patients with CRC and other types of cancers? It is because DR5 itself as a target is a pro-apoptotic receptor for TRAIL. TRAIL itself is widely expressed on malignant cells but rarely on healthy human cells, thus the first reason that makes it a good target.

Furthermore, TRAIL induces cell death via activation of the FADD and downstream caspase pathway. The tetravalent nature of ozekibart allows it to cluster this mode of action, potentially having a more profound effect of apoptotic activity or cell death [INHIBRX Investor Presentation November 2025, slide #19]. The point is that going after 4 DR5 molecules might ultimately do a better job at having a signaling effect compared to only targeting one DR5 molecule. Other advantages of ozekibart are its smaller size to penetrate tumor cells far more efficiently and lack of high-order clustering [developed to have an effector disabled Fc [domain region] and lack of anti-drug antibodies [thanks to removing immunogenic epitopes from the drug itself].

In order to see whether or not ozekibart in combination with FOLFIRI would be able to treat these patients with 3rd-line/4th-line advanced or metastatic, unresectable CRC, the company is in the process of running a phase 1/2 study. This particular trial, for this group of patients, is currently in an expansion cohort of 44 patients, as I mentioned above. First, it must be stated again that the patients recruited were those in dire shape, with at least 80% having received prior irinotecan. Along with them being in for 3rd-line and/or 4th-line therapy for their disease. Another aspect to consider is that the company had established these 44 patients as part of this expansion cohort into certain subpopulations depending upon what therapy is deployed. This can be shown as follows:

  • 4th-line therapy - consists of 70% of patients in this trial.
  • 3rd-line therapy - consists of 30% of patients in this trial.

The main efficacy endpoint of the most importance here would be progression-free survival [PFS]. This remains unknown, and the hope is that ozekibart + FOLFIRI can do a better job compared to FOLFIRI alone in regard to PFS. The company has seen some nice data though, showing that patients who received this ozekibart combination were able to achieve an overall response rate [ORR] of 23%.

This number seems quite low; how is it good? Well, when you compare it to the fact that the standard of care [SOC] is only able to show an ORR of between 5% and 6%, it makes this reported number very ideal. With respect to the catalyst for this program, the company believes that the PFS data can mature in Q2 of 2026. This would be PFS data from this phase 1/2 study using ozekibart + FOLFIRI for the treatment of these 3rd-line/4th-line advanced or metastatic, unresectable CRC. The hope is to have data then, but it is possible that the data may not be mature by then, and in that case the company would have to wait a bit longer to release this data.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Inhibrx had cash and cash equivalents of $153.1 million as of September 30, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand was because back in January of 2025 it entered into a 2025 Loan Agreement with Oxford Finance. Upon closing of this deal, Inhibrx had received $100 million in gross proceeds. Another positive item about this 5-year term loan facility is that it could allow the company to tap into another $50 million if it ends up wanting to do so.

For this part of the agreement, the company must request it, and then it is set at Oxford's sole discretion on whether or not it happens. The truth is that the company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, and this is not based on a projection of my own. This is based on the company's cash runway projection from its 10-Q SEC Filing. It believes that its cash on hand is enough to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months from the date of this SEC Filing.

The company burns roughly $33.8 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $28.5 million for R&D expenses and then $5.3 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Inhibrx. The first risk to consider would be in terms of its ongoing program using its best-in-class tetravalent DR5 agonist drug ozekibart + FOLFIRI to treat 3rd-line/4th-line advanced or metastatic, unresectable colorectal cancer [CRC]. The reason why is because the company is expected to provide updated PFS data in Q2 of 2026 [That's if the data matures enough by then but should still be released likely in 2026].

The risk here is that even though the company has shown a good ORR number, the goal is to show if this ozekibart combination can prevent progression of disease or death as well. There is no guarantee that this primary efficacy endpoint is going to be met in a statistically significant manner. Nor that this data will be good enough to move forward with in terms of phase 3 clinical testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expected BLA filing of ozekibart for the treatment of patients with unresectable or metastatic conventional chondrosarcoma. The BLA of ozekibart to treat these particular patients is expected in Q2 of 2026.

The risk here first and foremost is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to accept this U.S. regulatory application in its present form. Even if the FDA does end up accepting this NDA submission of ozekibart to treat these chondrosarcoma patients, there is no assurance that Inhibrx will ultimately receive FDA approval of it to treat these patients.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. As I have noted above in the financials, the company believes that its cash on hand will only be sufficient enough to fund its operations for at least 12-months from the date of its 10-Q SEC Filing, which was filed on November 14, 2025.

The risk here is that the company might need to raise additional cash as soon as possible, and this would dilute shareholders. Even if it needs to tap into the remaining $50 million under its 5-year term loan facility, I'm afraid that even this wouldn't be enough to carry it forward.

Conclusion

Inhibrx has done well to continue to develop its two drugs from its pipeline. What I went over above in great detail was that of its tetravalent DR5 agonist ozekibart. Specifically, the fact that progression-free survival [PFS] data is expected to be released possibly by Q2 of 2026 from an ongoing phase 1/2 study. This would be an early-stage trial using ozekibart in combination with FOLFIRI to treat 3rd-line/4th-line advanced or metastatic, unresectable, colorectal cancer patients. Although ORR data was good at 23% [far above the typical 5% to 6% observed for these patients], this is not the main primary endpoint being evaluated. It is that of PFS, and the hope is that the ozekibart + FOLFIRI combination beats FOLFIRI alone.

In addition, the company is also exploring the use of ozekibart in combination with irinotecan and temozolomide [IRI/TMZ] to treat advanced or metastatic, unresectable relapsed/refractory Ewing Sarcoma [ES] patients. Should the duration of response [DOR] and objective response rate [ORR] trends continue, then the company could possibly be in a place to meet with the FDA in the 2nd half of 2026 to discuss an Accelerated Approval pathway for this program. With everything mentioned above, I believe that it is important to keep a Strong Buy rating on this stock.
